itineraries.title: Northern Madagascar Road Trip: Tsingy, Three Bays & Nosy Be
itineraries.subtitle: From Diego Suarez to the Nosy Be archipelago: wild bays, stone forests, and turtle islands.
itineraries.notes: Avoid the rainy season (December to March): RN6 roads are sometimes cut off and there's cyclone risk. Access to Ankarana via the eastern entrance remains passable year-round. Check marine weather conditions before any island navigation. Final departure from Fascene Airport (Nosy Be), 20-30 minutes from Madirokely.

visits.text: Sakalava Bay is your starting point for the Three Bays hike. Reach it from Diego via a 5 km sandy track from the Ankorikely junction—you'll need a 4x4, as regular taxis won't go there. The bay is hammered by the Varatraza trade winds, making it one of the country's best kitesurfing spots. Set off early: the heat becomes intense after 10 AM on this shadeless coastline.

Between Sakalava Bay and Pigeons Bay, you follow dunes and xerophilous vegetation typical of the north—achypodiums and dry species growing between sand ridges. Dunes Bay offers a swim break in crystal-clear, rarely-visited waters. From the blockhouse overlooking it, you get a simultaneous view of both bays—stop for the photo.

Pigeons Bay closes the hike to the north, with whiter sand and calmer seas than Sakalava. This is where the water is most sheltered—perfect for a final swim before climbing back up to the lighthouse. Count on 3.5 to 4 hours of walking total from Sakalava, depending on your pace.

At Cap Miné, you reach the remains of French fortifications built from 1894 onwards to defend the main passage into Diego Suarez Bay. Four period cannons still stand in place, and the lighthouse lit on October 1, 1900 remains active. The view from this promontory over the bay and the pass is the region's finest—here you understand why the French navy valued this anchorage so highly. Heads up: the military collect a passage fee at the exit toward Ramena.

activities.text: The Three Bays coastal hike covers about 11 km from Sakalava to Ramena, with moderate elevation and some ridge trail sections. Bring plenty of water, a hat, and sunscreen—there's no shade on the ridges. From Ramena, a taxi takes you back to Diego via 18 km of paved road.

From Ramena late in the day, local operators offer short pirogue or boat trips to the Emerald Sea, a natural lagoon with shallow, translucent waters protected by a coral barrier. Mask and snorkel are all you need—you can stand for dozens of meters.

visits.text: Amber Mountain National Park is an anomaly in this arid north: an 18,200-hectare volcanic massif perched between 850 and 1,475 meters elevation that receives 3,500 mm of annual rainfall while Diego Suarez gets 1,000 mm. The result—a dense, cool cloud forest draped in orchids, tree ferns, and lianas, in total contrast to the surrounding dry savannas. Created in 1958, the park shelters 8 lemur species including the crowned lemur (Eulemur coronatus) and the rufous mouse lemur, one of the world's smallest primates. Arrive at the entrance office in Joffreville by 7 AM—guides are mandatory and the early hours are best for wildlife watching.

The Thousand Trees Trail crosses the park's rainforest alongside century-old trees draped in lianas and epiphytes. This circuit gives you the best chance of encountering Brookesia tuberculata, one of the planet's tiniest chameleons at under 5 cm. Your guide will spot it in the vegetation—observe and photograph, but don't touch. Furcifer timoni and Calumma vencesi, two recently scientifically described species, also live here.

The great Antomboka waterfall plunges 80 meters through the heart of the forest—it's one of the park's highlights, at the end of a 3 to 4-hour circuit from the entrance. Some routes also include the sacred Ampijoroana falls, where members of the Antakarana community leave offerings for wishes granted. Respect areas marked as sacred (fady) and always follow your guide's instructions.

activities.text: Choose your circuit based on your fitness: the short circuit (2h) reaches the small waterfall and Lake Mahasarika, the park's smallest lake, which supplies water to Diego Suarez city. The full circuit (5 to 6h) climbs to the great waterfall and viewpoints overlooking the Mozambique Channel. Pack waterproof shoes—the forest trails are often slippery.

In the evening at Joffreville, head out on a night walk with your guide. Chameleons and nocturnal lemurs like the rufous mouse lemur are active after dark and far easier to spot with a headlamp. Operators based in Joffreville organize these roughly 1.5-hour outings departing from village accommodations.

visits.text: 60 km south of Diego on the RN6, count on roughly 2 hours of driving before you spot the ochre canyon of the Red Tsingy of Irodo. This site resembles nothing else in Madagascar: unlike the limestone tsingy of Ankarana, these formations are sculpted from red sandstone, marl, and laterite by erosion from the Irodo River and rains. Hundreds of fairy chimneys in shades ranging from white to blood red, which the late-afternoon light sets ablaze—and since the site is ephemeral, each rainy season reshapes the sculptures. Two viewpoints by car overlook the canyon; a third on-foot access descends to the base of the formations. Bring water and a hat.

Continuing south on the RN6, the landscape gradually shifts: the red laterite yields to the first gray limestone formations and dry deciduous forests that announce the Ankarana massif. The 'Ankarana wall,' a limestone cliff stretching 25 km from north to south, looms on the horizon—it's a geological transition visible to the naked eye, two worlds separated by a few dozen kilometers.

activities.text: Visiting the Red Tsingy is largely done by car between viewpoints. To descend into the heart of the fairy chimneys, count on 45 minutes on foot from the third access. The best light is late afternoon: if your timing allows, organize your departure from Diego to arrive on site around 4 PM.

visits.text: The Ankarana Special Reserve (18,220 ha, created in 1956, UNESCO-listed in September 2023) shelters Africa's largest cave network: 110 km of underground cavities carved into Jurassic limestone that emerged from the seas 100 million years ago. Above ground, the tsingy—the Malagasy word means 'walking on tiptoes'—form a labyrinth of razor-sharp limestone pinnacles, deep canyons, and crevasses. Stop at the Mahamasina reception office before entering—a guide is mandatory. The site is 108 km south of Antsiranana.

The lost rivers are one of Ankarana's most spectacular phenomena: a green-water river briefly crosses a canyon before disappearing under the rock to join the underground network. This circuit (about 4 km in 3 hours, with a passage through shallow water) takes you into the caves to observe cave-dwelling species—some are blind and unpigmented. Milaintety Cave at the circuit's end features a natural pool surrounded by ferns.

Two suspension bridges connect the viewpoints above the great canyon of the Benavony loop, offering a plunging view over the stone forest and crevasses. From the high point at 206 m, on clear days you glimpse the islets of the Mozambique Channel. A great baobab 8 m in circumference awaits you at the circuit's end. Ankarana's dry forest shelters three endemic baobab species: Adansonia madagascariensis, Adansonia perrieri, and Adansonia suarezensis.

activities.text: Eight circuits are marked in the reserve, from 3 km to 10 km, from 2 hours to a full day. The Benavony loop (5 km, 3 to 4h) is the most complete: tsingy, two suspension bridges, a cave with concretions, and a panoramic viewpoint. For the lost rivers (4 km, 3h), bring shoes that can get wet. Pack a headlamp for the caves.

Some Ankarana caves shelter the royal tombs of the Antakarana, who took refuge in the massif in the 19th century to flee Merina armies. These caves are sacred and their access forbidden to Merina descendants—a history of conflicts between Malagasy kingdoms still very present in local memory. Ask your guide to tell you this story along the way.

visits.text: Ambanja is the capital of the 'valley of perfumes': the Sambirano Valley produces cocoa, coffee, vanilla, vetiver, and ylang-ylang. If you pass through on a weekday, the local market is worth the detour—spices, tropical fruits, 100% local atmosphere. The town's cathedral hides an anecdote: its metal framework comes from the former Strasbourg train station. The town is 18 km from Ankify port.

Ankify is the only embarkation point on the northern mainland coast for reaching Nosy Be. A small fishing village, it's above all a transit point. The speedboat to Nosy Be takes 45 minutes. Check schedules in advance—there may be waiting time depending on traffic. The crossing is generally calm between May and November.

Hell-Ville (Andoany) is the main port and only true town on Nosy Be, named after Admiral Louis de Hell, governor of Réunion, who obtained the island's cession in 1840. From the landing dock, a first scent of ylang-ylang floats in the hot air. Don't linger too much tonight—head to Madirokely, fifteen minutes away, to settle in facing the beach.

activities.text: The road between Ankarana and Ankify (about 140 km, 2.5 to 3h) first follows the dry forest then descends toward the coast passing through Ambilobe and Ambanja. The RN6's quality is uneven on some sections—leave early to have time to see Ambanja before boarding.

The speedboat crossing Ankify → Hell-Ville lasts 45 minutes. If the weather is favorable, you'll spot Nosy Be's verdant coasts long before arriving. Outside the dry season, the sea can be rough—bring a plastic bag if you're prone to seasickness.

Late in the day at Madirokely, enjoy Nosy Be's main beach to decompress after the long road and sea crossing. It's a wide strip of sand lively in the evening, with beachfront restaurants. You're staying here three nights—get comfortably settled.

visits.text: Mont Passot rises to 329 meters at Nosy Be's center and offers the island's best panoramic view: eleven volcanic lakes appear below, framed by tropical vegetation, with the ocean on the horizon. Go in late afternoon—it's one of the archipelago's most beautiful sunsets.

The Lokobe Nature Reserve is the last remnant of primary forest on Nosy Be. It protects the black macaco lemur, the panther chameleon (Furcifer pardalis) with its dazzling colors, and great trees like the Malagasy canarium and rosewood. Access is by pirogue from the southeast coast—count on a half-day with a mandatory guide.

The sacred tree of Mahatsinjo, a several-centuries-old Ficus bengalensis with spectacular aerial roots, is a Sakalava worship site. This ancient witness to the island's history can be visited in a few minutes but merits the detour to understand local spirituality.

activities.text: Andilana beach, in the island's north, is Nosy Be's most beautiful: crystalline waters, immaculate white sand, lightly frequented. Plan a half-day—count on 45 minutes' drive from Madirokely.
In the evening, take a tour of Hell-Ville's covered market: local spices, ylang-ylang, vanilla, pepper. It's the best place to bring back olfactory souvenirs from the perfume island.

visits.text: Nosy Komba, nicknamed 'lemur island,' is a volcanic island without roads or cars, 30 minutes by boat from Nosy Be. The village of Ampangorina welcomes visitors with its craft workshops: hand-stitched embroidered tablecloths sewn by village women, rosewood carvings. Don't miss the lemur park—the black macaques are easily approached and live in matriarchal groups led by females.

Nosy Tanikely became a marine national park in 2010—fishing is prohibited within a 700-meter radius around the lighthouse. Result: an astonishing density of fish right from the beach edge, well-preserved corals, seagrass beds where you'll almost certainly encounter green turtles. The interesting depths start a few meters from the beach—a beginner with mask and snorkel sees as much as an experienced diver.

activities.text: Full-day excursion from Madirokely: Nosy Komba in the morning (lemurs and crafts), Nosy Tanikely in the afternoon (snorkeling). Local operators offer this combo on shared or private boat. You return to sleep at Madirokely in the evening—no need to move luggage.

Snorkeling on Nosy Tanikely's reef: the depths descend gradually from 2 to 12 meters from the beach. You'll observe snappers, clownfish, barracudas, giant clams, and green turtles in the seagrass beds. Diadema sea urchins are present—don't touch the bottom.

practical_info.conseil: Best conditions between May and December

map_keywords: Nosy Sakatia Orchid Island Nosy Be

visits.text: Nosy Sakatia, nicknamed 'orchid island,' is a small preserved island west of Nosy Be, accessible in 5 minutes by boat from Chanty Beach. Less frequented than Tanikely, it charms with its forest trails, wild orchids, and chameleons. Antagnatrozona Bay shelters vast seagrass beds well protected from swells—this is where green turtles come to feed.

activities.text: Morning snorkeling on Nosy Sakatia's seagrass beds: depth of 2 to 3 meters, clear water, green turtles almost guaranteed. Let yourself drift slowly above the seagrass—they'll approach on their own if you remain still. Return to Madirokely early afternoon.
Coastal walk on foot around Nosy Sakatia, from cove to cove: the forest trails cross dense vegetation where wild orchids grow on tree trunks. Bring good shoes—the terrain is uneven.

In the afternoon, prepare for tomorrow's Nosy Iranja expedition: check tide times with your operator (the 1.2 km sandbar is only accessible at low tide), confirm the bivouac, and lighten your luggage to the maximum for two nights on the island.

map_keywords: Tombolo Nosy Iranja banc de sable

visits.text: Nosy Iranja is actually two islands connected by a 1.2 km white sand tombolo—at low tide, you walk between two turquoise oceans on this sand tongue that completely disappears at high tide. The large island, Nosy Iranja Be (200 ha), shelters a fishing village and a lighthouse built in 1909 by Gustave Eiffel's workshop, still visible from offshore. The small one, Nosy Iranja Kely (13 ha), is Madagascar's main hawksbill turtle nesting site.

The fishing village of Nosy Iranja Be deserves a stroll: stilt houses built with mangrove wood and traveler's palm leaves, small grocery stores, fruit and fritter stands. Climb the Eiffel lighthouse (20 m, 1909) for a 360° view over both islets and the tombolo—this is where Nosy Iranja's most famous photo was taken.

activities.text: Navigation from Madirokely to Nosy Iranja takes 1.5 to 2 hours depending on conditions. Leave early morning to arrive at low tide and walk the tombolo upon arrival. Your bivouac luggage awaits you on the island—you won't have to reload anything onto a boat tonight.

Snorkeling at the foot of Nosy Iranja Kely: fish-rich reefs, surface turtles, and in season (August-November) humpback whales spotted offshore. The water is between 27 and 29°C year-round. In the evening, if you're bivouacking, local operators organize night outings to observe turtles laying eggs on Nosy Iranja Kely's beach—a rare experience.

visits.text: The second day at Nosy Iranja is the tombolo without the day-tripper crowds—who leave around 3 PM. At low tide, you have the sand tongue to yourself late afternoon and at sunrise. Nosy Iranja Kely, the small 13-ha island, can be crossed on foot in about twenty minutes between ferns and endemic species.

activities.text: If turtles nested the previous night, you may observe baby hatchlings making their way to the sea at sunrise—the island's most moving moment. Ask your operator to alert you if a nest is hatching: it's random depending on season but frequent between October and February.

Free snorkeling on the reefs west of Nosy Iranja Kely, where tourist pressure is zero once the boats have left. Enjoy the second day's calm to explore at your own pace—that's the advantage of bivouacking over a day trip.
Late afternoon, walk on the tombolo at sunset. The raking light on the white sand and turquoise waters is the day's most photogenic. Check the tide to be sure the tombolo is still exposed.

visits.text: Russian Bay, a stopover on the return to Nosy Be, is a sheltered cove on the mainland still very lightly visited. The site takes its name from Admiral Rojdestvensky's fleet that took refuge here in 1905 during the Russo-Japanese War—the camp ruins are still visible. Near-deserted white sand beaches and turtle nests dot the shore.

activities.text: Navigation from Nosy Iranja to Russian Bay (~30 km, 1h by sea) hugging the wild mainland coast. Short stopover for a swim or walk on the deserted beaches, then ascent to Nosy Be for the night. Spinner dolphins are frequent on this route.
Return to Madirokely late afternoon—find your base hotel and luggage left on the island. Last evening on Madirokely beach before departure.

visits.text: Hell-Ville (Andoany), 20 minutes from Madirokely, deserves a morning stop before the flight: the central market gathers local spices, vanilla, ylang-ylang in bottles, and handicrafts. This is where the island's scent is most intense. The colonial waterfront with its old naval cannons and period buildings also merits a quick tour.

activities.text: Last shopping at Hell-Ville market in the morning, then transfer to Fascene Airport east of the island—count on 20 to 30 minutes' drive from Madirokely or Hell-Ville depending on your hotel. The airport serves Antananarivo on regular flights and some seasonal international connections (Réunion, Addis Ababa, Milan).

seo.h1_title: Northern Madagascar Road Trip: Tsingy, Three Bays & Nosy Be
seo.intro_paragraph: Start from Diego Suarez—one of the world's most beautiful bays—for a coastal hike between three wild bays, before plunging into the cloud forests of Amber Mountain and the razor-sharp stone labyrinth of Ankarana. What follows unfolds at sea: speedboat to Nosy Be, cruising between deserted islands, Nosy Iranja's white sand tombolo, and sea turtles in turquoise waters. An itinerary that chains together spectacular geology, endemic wildlife, and island paradise over twelve days.
seo.seo_hook: Razor-sharp tsingy emerged from the seas 100 million years ago, emerald bays guarded by colonial cannons, hawksbill turtles on a sand bar that vanishes at high tide. Northern Madagascar resembles nowhere else on Earth.
featured_list: Diego Suarez: Three Bays hike between turquoise sea and 19th-century fortifications
Ankarana: 110 km of underground caves beneath 18,000 hectares of UNESCO-listed tsingy
Nosy Be: archipelago of deserted islands, green turtles in seagrass beds, and ylang-ylang
Nosy Iranja: 1.2 km tombolo connecting two islets, Eiffel lighthouse (1909), and nesting site

places_taglines.MG::antsiranana: Where a natural harbor ranks among the world's finest deep-water anchorages
places_taglines.MG::joffreville: Gateway to Amber Mountain's mist-wrapped forest—a cloud island in the arid north
places_taglines.MG::ankarana-mahamasina: Africa's largest cave network lies beneath limestone pinnacles sharp as blades
places_taglines.MG::madirokely: Nosy Be's main beach, where the scent of ylang-ylang drifts on every breeze
places_taglines.MG::tanikely: A marine park since 2010, where turtles glide through crystal shallows meters from shore
places_taglines.MG::sakatia: Wild orchids cling to forest trunks above seagrass meadows where green turtles graze
places_taglines.MG::iranja: Two islands joined by a white sand ribbon—Madagascar's premier hawksbill nesting ground
places_taglines.MG::baie-des-russes: A forgotten cove where a Russian fleet once sheltered from the tides of war

highlights: Ankarana Special Reserve: 110 km of caves and limestone tsingy UNESCO-listed in 2023
Nosy Iranja: 1.2 km tombolo and 1909 Eiffel lighthouse, Madagascar's main hawksbill turtle nesting site
Amber Mountain: cloud forest at 1,475 m sheltering the world's smallest chameleon (Brookesia tuberculata)
Nosy Tanikely: marine park protected since 2010, green turtles in seagrass beds accessible snorkeling right from the beach

itineraries.title: Classic Madagascar Grand Tour: RN7, Baobabs, Pangalanes & Sainte-Marie Island
itineraries.subtitle: From the capital to the Isalo canyons, from giant baobabs of the west coast to lemur forests of the east, all the way to the beaches and whales of Sainte-Marie.
itineraries.notes: Domestic flights essential between Tuléar and Morondava - this cross-country connection can be unreliable, so verify schedules before booking. Return flight from Sainte-Marie to Tana at trip's end. 4x4 with driver strongly recommended on the RN7. Build in flexibility for transit days.

visits.text: Climb up to Rova Manjakamiadana first thing in the morning - perched 200 meters above Tana on Analamanga hill, it's the only spot where you can take in the entire capital in a single sweep. French adventurer Jean Laborde clad it in stone between 1839 and 1841 for Queen Ranavalona I; a 1995 fire gutted the interiors, since restored and reopened. Allow 90 minutes - and ignore the touts at the entrance.

The Upper Town (Tanàna Ambony) is classified as a protected historic zone - unique in Madagascar. Its steep cobbled lanes and red-brick houses resemble an old European quarter catapulted into the Indian Ocean. Climb to the viewpoint late afternoon: Tana ignites under golden light.

Take a spin through Andravoahangy market - it's the capital's largest and most chaotic. Spices, filigree silver jewelry, spare parts, live poultry. Go in the morning before 10am, when the stalls are still full.

activities.text: Wander on foot through the Upper Town's alleyways in early evening - locals heading home, kids playing on the staircases. It's the best time to feel Tana's rhythm without a guide.

visits.text: Make the drive to Ambohimanga, 21 km north of Tana - count on an hour with traffic. This isn't your ordinary tourist site: Malagasy people still come here on pilgrimage to make wishes. Inscribed as UNESCO World Heritage in 2001, the royal city closes with a 12-ton stone disc. The Mahandrihono palace in rosewood was home to King Andrianampoinimerina - the one who dreamed of a kingdom whose only limits would be the sea.

Look for the two intertwined trees on a rock beside the sacred lake - legend says they shelter the souls of two cursed royal-era lovers and would bleed if separated. Don't point at sacred sites with your finger: fold it inward, as locals will show you.

activities.text: Walk the trail through the residual forest around the Rova - one of the last fragments of primary deciduous forest from Madagascar's interior. Your local guide will explain traditional uses of endemic plant species.

visits.text: Welcome to Antsirabe - the town that smells of sulfur and minerals. Perched at 1,500 m on a volcanic plateau, it draws its thermal water from soil so loaded with mineral salts it earned the nickname "where there is much salt." Its wide colonial avenues swarm with brightly painted pousse-pousse - grab one to cross town: it's the quintessential local transport.

Lake Tritriva deserves the detour: this volcanic crater 160 meters deep with dark blue-green waters is considered sacred. Don't eat pork before you go - villagers strongly advise against it and the legend of a tourist who drowned for ignoring this taboo still circulates. Seen from above, the lake has exactly the shape of Madagascar. Allow 20 minutes to walk around it.

On the way, stop at Lake Andraikiba, 7 km from town - more accessible and more lively: fishermen beat the water with bundles of sticks to stun fish, women wash clothes on the banks. A peaceful setting to catch your breath.

activities.text: Visit a zebu horn or semi-precious stone workshop in the center - artisans work in front of you and sell directly. It's two to three times cheaper than in Tana.
Take a pousse-pousse ride through downtown streets - negotiate directly with the driver. It's one of the most useful gestures for the local economy.

visits.text: Push open the door to a workshop in Ambositra - you're entering the world of the Zafimaniry, whose woodcraft is inscribed on UNESCO's intangible heritage list. No nails, no screws: panels carved with geometric motifs fit together by hand, following rules transmitted orally from father to son for centuries. The same motifs cover walls, doors and furniture of houses - it's a visual language.
If you have time, take the track to the village of Antoetra - there you'll see Zafimaniry work in its real context, in an actual house, with the real tools. Not in a tourist shop.

activities.text: Browse Ambositra's craft markets and buy directly from producers - marquetry, wood carvings, textiles. Prices are well below Tana's for the same pieces.

visits.text: Climb into Fianarantsoa's upper town late afternoon - its red-brick lanes stacked on multiple hills are classified as a protected zone, unique in Madagascar. Climb to the upper viewpoint: the vista of terraced rice paddies tumbling into the valley is one of the finest in the highlands. Bring good shoes - the cobbles are slippery after rain.

Don't leave without tasting the local wine - Fianarantsoa is home to Madagascar's only vineyard, produced on the surrounding slopes. The red and white have a very particular profile, far from European standards: that's exactly what makes them interesting. Look for the cellar in town.

activities.text: Stroll the upper town's lanes in the evening when raking light colors the red bricks and schoolkids head home. It's free, quiet and a hundred times truer than any guided tour.

visits.text: 12 km south of Ambalavao on the RN7, stop at Anja reserve - managed directly by villagers since 1999, no national park or large structure. They protect around 400 ring-tailed lemurs here, the ones with the long black-and-white striped tail. Arrive early: in the morning, the makis are active and unafraid - they sometimes approach within a meter. The profits have funded a village school. Allow 2 hours for the small loop.

Stop by an Antemoro paper workshop in Ambalavao - they make pulp from the bark of the avoha shrub, embedding dried flowers and leaves. The result resembles floral parchment. You can watch every step and leave with sheets made on site.

If you're there on a Wednesday, don't miss the zebu market - 2,000 head of cattle transit through this town every week. The herders draped in colorful blankets, the bargaining ritual and the auctions are a spectacle in themselves.

activities.text: Guided circuit in Anja reserve - the local guide will also show you chameleons, boas and endemic birds tucked among the rocks that you'd never have spotted alone.

visits.text: Stop at the Maison de l'Isalo, 9 km from Ranohira on the RN7 - it's the best starting point before entering the park. In 45 minutes, you'll grasp the essentials: the massif is formed of Jurassic sandstone 250 million years old, sculpted into canyons and pinnacles by erosion. The rupestral flora around you - elephant-foot pachypodiums, aloes, euphorbias - grows nowhere else.
Go see the Isalo Window late in the day - this natural arch carved in pink sandstone is the park's iconic sunset spot. Arrive 30 minutes before sunset to secure a good angle and avoid the rush.

activities.text: Late afternoon, do a first short 2-hour hike around Ranohira with a guide hired at the park office - mandatory. The golden light on the reliefs at day's end alone justifies the trip.

visits.text: Set off early for the Canyon des Makis - the sun heats up fast and lemurs hide after 9am. You'll find seven species here including the ring-tailed maki and Verreaux's sifaka, which dances rather than walks. The canyon is deep, shaded, with dense vegetation at the bottom - a total contrast to the plateau's aridity you just crossed.

At the canyon's end, you emerge at the natural pool - a rocky basin fed by a waterfall, fresh and transparent water. Dive in: after hours of walking under the southern sun, it's your reward. The blue pool, a bit farther, is tinted mineral blue-green and deeper. Check conditions with your guide.

activities.text: Major hike Canyon des Makis → Nymphs' Waterfall → black pool → blue pool. Plan 4 to 6 hours depending on your pace, minimum 2 liters of water per person, hat and hiking boots. It's the best day's walk of the entire RN7.

visits.text: Ifaty marks the end of the RN7 - and the beginning of the sea. Forty kilometers north of Tuléar, its white sand beaches are protected by a coral reef that calms coastal waters. After days on the road and track, head straight to the beach upon arrival: cold water on tired legs is the best reward the west coast can offer.

Very early in the morning, watch the Vezo fishermen's pirogues return with their night's catch. This nomadic sea people build their outrigger pirogues on site, right on the sand. Don't miss the improvised shipyards at the beach's edge.

activities.text: Snorkeling on Ifaty's coral reef - one of the few still-preserved reefs on Madagascar's west coast. Local operators offer mask and snorkel or pirogue outings to the coral heads.

map_keywords: Allée des Baobabs Morondava coucher de soleil

visits.text: You've seen photos of the Avenue of the Baobabs thousands of times - in real life, it's even more striking. 17 km north of Morondava, a row of Adansonia grandidieri - the most imposing baobab species, endemic to the west coast - raise their silvery trunks 30 m high and 3 m in diameter against a sky that catches fire. These trees are between 300 and 800 years old. Come at sunset - and arrive 30 minutes early to choose your angle.

activities.text: Domestic flight Tuléar → Morondava - check schedules 48 hours before departure. Cross-country connections in Madagascar are notorious for unannounced cancellations. Always have a plan B.
Evening stroll through the coastal village of Mangily - first contact with the west coast after the flight: sandy beach, pirogues hauled up on sand, slow atmosphere opposite the highlands.

visits.text: The sandy track from Morondava to Belo-sur-Mer takes about 5 hours by 4x4 - it crosses baobab forests, fords and nameless villages off the map. Leave at dawn: the track heats up fast and fords are more passable early morning.

In Belo-sur-Mer, head straight to the beachside shipyards. Marine carpenters assemble dhows and pirogues the old way - no plans, no machines, by hand and eye, with local hardwoods. Stay a while and watch them work without asking anything.

activities.text: Sailing pirogue outing on the lagoon late afternoon - the waters are turquoise and calm, fishermen returning with their catch. A local operator will take you for a few thousand ariary.

visits.text: This second day in Belo-sur-Mer is the circuit's real luxury - you're not required to go anywhere. Wander the quay at sunrise, watch dhows leave for the day, chat with pirogue builders. The village has no set program. That's its charm.

activities.text: Pirogue excursion to Andravoho island to explore the lagoon's seabed - corals, tropical fish and calm waters. Arrange the outing with your host the evening before.

visits.text: Buffer day in Tana after the Morondava → Antananarivo flight. If the flight is early morning, use the afternoon for the Museum of Art and Archaeology in the Isoraka district - collections of beads, ceramics and ritual objects that tell Madagascar's story better than any guidebook. Often empty of tourists, which is an advantage.

activities.text: Return flight Morondava → Antananarivo - reconfirm ticket 48 hours before. Allow a one-day margin if you have an international flight within 24 hours.

visits.text: Stop at Marozevo on the road from Tana to visit the Peyrieras reserve - a breeding center for giant butterflies, chameleons and endemic frogs. Takes 45 minutes and provides good preparation for the Andasibe forest.

In Andasibe, enter the Analamazaotra reserve at opening with your local guide - mandatory and essential. You're here for the indri-indri: the world's largest lemur, 70 cm, 7 kg, capable of jumping 10 meters from tree to tree. Its call - deep and powerful, carrying several kilometers - awakens the forest at dawn. If you hear it before seeing it, follow the sound. The forest also shelters 11 other lemur species and extremely rare Eulophiella orchids.

activities.text: Night forest walk by flashlight - dwarf and mouse lemurs with shining eyes in the branches. Arrange the outing the same morning at the park entrance or through your accommodation.

visits.text: From Manambato, embark on the Pangalanes Canal - 665 km of waterway running along the east coast, laid out by General Gallieni. Vanilla, coffee and clove plantations line the banks; merchant pirogues still use it daily. The journey to Akanin'ny Nofy takes 1h30: watch local life unfold from the boat.

The Palmarium reserve, on the shores of Lake Ampitabe, shelters a dozen semi-free lemur species - some very tame and approaching within arm's reach. Don't feed them. The flora is equally remarkable: orchids, carnivorous plants, extremely rare endemic palms including Voanioala and Lemurophoenix, found nowhere else.

activities.text: Night boat outing to Rooster Island to observe the Aye-Aye - this very rare nocturnal lemur accumulates oddities: bat ears, skeletal fingers including one very long one for extracting larvae from trunks, hallucinated stare. Departure around 5:30pm, duration about 1 hour. Arrange through your accommodation.

visits.text: Cross Lake Ampitabe by boat to visit a Betsimisaraka village on the opposite shore. No staging - just a direct encounter with a community living off fishing and rice, cut off from the rest of the world by water. Take time for a meal on site if you're invited.

activities.text: Free day on the lakeshore - swimming from the white sand beach, kayaking, hammock or walk through the coastal forest. Akanin'ny Nofy means "nest of dreams" in Malagasy: that's exactly it.

visits.text: Morning boat navigation from Akanin'ny Nofy to Tamatave (Toamasina) - about 3 hours on the canal. The city is the great eastern port: its palm-lined avenues and faded colonial houses recall that the east coast long looked toward the Indian Ocean more than the highlands. Stop for a meal at the market before heading north.

Mahambo, 60 km north of Tamatave, is your airlock before Sainte-Marie. Coconut groves, beach exposed to Indian Ocean swells, some waves for surfers. It's simple and honest - use it to rest before the crossing.

activities.text: Swimming or surfing at Mahambo depending on conditions - the east coast is exposed to Indian Ocean swells. Check conditions with your accommodation before jumping in the water.

visits.text: Upon arrival in Sainte-Marie, start with Ambodifotatra, the main village - its markets, old church and slow, francophone atmosphere set the tone. Sainte-Marie measures 49 km long by 5 km wide: there's no possible rush here.

Go to the pirates' cemetery in Forbans Bay - accessible on foot at low tide, by pirogue at high tide. Tombstones from the 1830s, some carved with a classic skull and crossbones, mark the graves of William Kidd, Thomas Tew, Olivier Levasseur called La Buse and other pirates who made this island their lair between 1680 and 1720. It's one of the rare authentic pirate cemeteries in the world.

activities.text: Ferry crossing from Soanierana-Ivongo - reconfirm schedules the day before. The Gasikara Be can be cancelled due to swells: always keep flexibility around this crossing. In case of cancellation, short flights from Tamatave or Tana exist as alternatives.

practical_info.booking: Book in advance, reconfirm day before

visits.text: If you're here between July and September, don't miss the humpback whales - they migrate from Antarctica, 5,000 km, to breed in the Sainte-Marie channel. Peak is in August: 198 individuals were counted in 12 hours from shore during one census. Off season, head to the natural pools of Ambodiatafana in the island's north: granite rocks form basins filled by tides, perfect for swimming.

Make a detour through the Ikalalao forest (400 ha) - three endemic lemur species, chameleons and the Eulophiella roempleriana, the giant orchid nicknamed the Queen of Madagascar, found only on this island.

activities.text: Boat outing for whale watching (July-September) - choose a local operator who respects regulatory distances. Don't dive from the boat near them. Off season, go coastline cruising on the west coast by pirogue instead.

map_keywords: Island of Palms Nosy Nanto Pirogue
White Sand Beach Island of Palms Snorkeling

visits.text: Take a pirogue from southern Sainte-Marie - 10 minutes and 400 meters of sea separate the two islands. Île aux Nattes measures 2.5 km by 1.5 km: no cars, trails through coconut palms, a white sand beach at trail's end. After three weeks on the road, settle in and do nothing. That's the objective.
The island's small lemur park shelters ring-tailed makis in forest and some specimens of Eulophiella roempleriana - the giant endemic orchid. A quiet visit, no crowds, no program.

activities.text: Walk around the island - allow 2 hours taking your time on interior trails. You'll encounter three residents, two chickens and a chameleon.
Snorkeling in the southern lagoon - crystal-clear waters, preserved coral reefs and no other tourists. Mask and snorkel to borrow or bring.

visits.text: Last day in Tana after the flight from Sainte-Marie. If you have a few hours before your international flight, hit the Digue craft market (Lisy) - silver jewelry, zebu horn lamps, embroidery, basketry. It's the last chance to leave with something made in Madagascar rather than imported from China.

activities.text: Return flight Sainte-Marie → Antananarivo (1h) - to combine with your international flight according to schedules. Book ahead: seats are limited in high season.

practical_info.booking: Book in advance, seats limited in high season

seo.h1_title: Classic Madagascar Grand Tour: RN7, Baobabs, Pangalanes & Sainte-Marie Island
seo.intro_paragraph: From Antananarivo to the golden canyons of Isalo, from 800-year-old baobabs in Morondava to the lemur forests of Andasibe, this circuit crosses Madagascar from end to end in 21 days. The RN7, the Pangalanes Canal and Sainte-Marie Island compose the classic grand tour of the Great Island - the one travelers do once in a lifetime.
seo.seo_hook: Wake up to lemurs in Andasibe, sandstone canyons in Isalo, 800-year-old baobabs in Morondava and humpback whales in Sainte-Marie: Madagascar in all its excess in 21 days.
featured_list: Isalo: 250-million-year-old Jurassic sandstone canyons and turquoise natural pools
Morondava: Adansonia grandidieri 300 to 800 years old with trunks 3 meters in diameter
Andasibe: primary forest and the indri-indri's call carrying several kilometers at dawn
Sainte-Marie: up to 198 humpback whales counted in 12 hours from shore in peak season

places_taglines.MG::antananarivo: Capital of a thousand royal hills where markets pulse with the island's red earth colors
places_taglines.MG::antsirabe: Thermal town at 1,500m with volcanic crater lakes and rainbow-painted pousse-pousse
places_taglines.MG::ambositra: Capital of UNESCO-listed Zafimaniry woodcraft assembled without a single nail
places_taglines.MG::fianarantsoa: Colonial upper town in red brick and Madagascar's only vineyard terraces
places_taglines.MG::ambalavao: Village reserve protecting 400 ring-tailed lemurs and workshops crafting floral Antemoro paper
places_taglines.MG::ranohira: Gateway to Isalo park where 250-million-year-old Jurassic sandstone shapes 81,540 hectares
places_taglines.MG::ifaty: Beach resort on a preserved coral reef where Vezo sea nomads build pirogues on sand
places_taglines.MG::morondava: Where ancient Adansonia grandidieri catch fire under sunset skies
places_taglines.MG::belo-sur-mer: Isolated village of master shipwrights and turquoise lagoon at road's sandy end
places_taglines.MG::andasibe: Sanctuary of the indri-indri whose haunting call awakens the primary forest at dawn
places_taglines.MG::akanin-ny-nofy: Nest of dreams on the Pangalanes Canal between freshwater and dense eastern forest
places_taglines.MG::mahambo: Coastal surf village and coconut groves facing Indian Ocean swells
places_taglines.MG::ile-sainte-marie: Pirate island where humpback whales migrate from Antarctica in austral summer
places_taglines.MG::ile-aux-nattes: Car-free islet of 2.5 km to decompress at circuit's end with feet in water

highlights: Isalo Park: 81,540 ha of Jurassic sandstone, 7 lemur species and natural pools at canyon bottoms
Avenue of Baobabs: Endemic Adansonia grandidieri 300 to 800 years old, trunks reaching 3 m diameter
Andasibe: The indri-indri, world's largest lemur (70 cm, 7 kg), call carrying several km through forest
Sainte-Marie: Up to 198 humpback whales counted in 12 hours from shore in peak season (August)

itineraries.title: Madagascar's Central Highlands: Culture, Crafts and RN7 Rainforests
itineraries.subtitle: A loop from Antananarivo through royal hills, Zafimaniry workshops, Ranomafana lemurs and highland rice terraces - no flights, no rush.
itineraries.notes: Entirely road-based circuit, zero internal flights. Suitable for families and seniors. Recommend a 4x4 with driver for sections between Ambositra and Ranomafana. Roads passable year-round but prefer the dry season (April–October).

visits.text: Head up to Rova Manjakamiadana first thing in the morning - perched 200 metres above Tana on Analamanga hill, it's the only spot where you can take in the entire capital in a single sweep. Jean Laborde, a French adventurer, wrapped it in stone between 1839 and 1841 for Queen Ranavalona I; a 1995 fire gutted the interiors, which have since been restored and reopened. Allow 90 minutes - and ignore the hawkers at the entrance.

The Upper Town (Tanàna Ambony) is classified as a protected heritage zone - unique in Madagascar. Its sloping cobbled lanes, red-brick houses and cascading staircases feel like an old European quarter dropped into the Indian Ocean. Climb up to the viewpoint late afternoon: the golden light on the rooftops is worth the climb.
Drop by Andravoahangy market - the largest and most chaotic in the capital. Spices, filigree silver jewellery, spare parts, live poultry. Go in the morning before 10am, when the stalls are full.

activities.text: Wander the lanes of the Upper Town on foot in early evening - locals heading home, children playing on the stairs, colonial houses lighting their windows. The best time to feel Tana's rhythm without a guide.

visits.text: Make the drive to Ambohimanga, 21 km north of Tana - allow an hour in traffic. This isn't your average tourist site: Malagasy people still come here on pilgrimage to make wishes. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2001, the royal city is sealed by a 12-tonne stone disc. The Mahandrihono palace, built of rosewood, was home to King Andrianampoinimerina - the one who dreamed of a kingdom bounded only by the sea.

Look for the two trees entwined on a rock beside the sacred lake - legend has it they shelter the souls of two star-crossed royal-era lovers and would bleed if severed. Don't point your finger at the sacred sites: fold your index finger down, as the locals will explain.

Late afternoon, push open the door of the Museum of Art and Archaeology in the Isoraka neighbourhood in Tana - collections of beads, ceramics and ritual objects that tell Madagascar's story better than any guidebook. Often empty of tourists, which is a bonus.

activities.text: Walk the trail through the residual forest around Ambohimanga's Rova - one of the last fragments of primary deciduous forest in inland Madagascar. The local guide will explain the traditional uses of endemic plant species.

visits.text: Welcome to Antsirabe - the town that smells of sulphur and minerals. Perched at 1,500 m on a volcanic plateau, it draws its thermal water from ground so salt-laden it earned the nickname 'where there's a lot of salt'. Its colonial avenues swarm with brightly painted pousse-pousse - hop into one to cross town: it's the local transport of choice.

Lake Tritriva deserves the detour: this 160-metre-deep volcanic crater with dark blue-green waters is sacred. Don't eat pork before you go - villagers strongly advise against it and the legend of a tourist drowned for ignoring this taboo still circulates. From above, the lake is shaped exactly like Madagascar. Allow 20 minutes to walk around it.

On the way, stop at Lake Andraikiba, 7 km from town - more accessible and livelier: fishermen striking the water with bundles of sticks to stun the fish, women washing clothes on the banks. A peaceful spot to catch your breath.

activities.text: Visit a semi-precious stone workshop in central Antsirabe - the artisans work in front of you and sell directly, at prices far lower than in Tana. Antsirabe is known across the island for its surrounding quarries.
Take a pousse-pousse ride through the town centre streets - negotiate directly with the driver. It's a tangible gesture for the local economy.

visits.text: Step into a workshop in Ambositra - you're entering the world of the Zafimaniry, whose woodcraft is inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Heritage list. No nails, no screws: the panels carved with geometric motifs fit together by hand, following rules passed orally from father to son for centuries. The same patterns cover walls, doors and furniture in the houses - it's a visual language.

Take the track to the village of Antoetra - there you'll see Zafimaniry work in its real context: in an actual house, with the actual tools, pieces that aren't made for tourists. The track is bumpy but passable by 4x4.

activities.text: Tour Ambositra's craft markets and buy directly from the makers - marquetry, wood carvings, textiles. Prices are far lower than in Tana for the same pieces.

visits.text: Climb into Fianarantsoa's upper town late afternoon - its red-brick lanes stacked across several hills are classified as a protected zone, unique in Madagascar. Head up to the upper viewpoint: the view over the terraced rice paddies tumbling into the valley is among the finest in the highlands. Bring good shoes - the cobblestones are slippery after rain.

Don't leave without tasting the local wine - Fianarantsoa is home to Madagascar's only vineyard, grown on the surrounding slopes. The red and white have a very particular profile, far from European standards: that's exactly what makes them interesting. Look for the cellar in town.

activities.text: Stroll the upper town's lanes in the evening when the low light colours the red bricks and schoolchildren head home. It's free, quiet and a hundred times more genuine than any guided tour.

map_keywords: Réserve Communautaire Anja Ambalavao lémuriens catta

visits.text: Twelve kilometres south of Ambalavao on the RN7, stop at Anja Reserve - run directly by the villagers since 1999. They protect around 400 ring-tailed lemurs here, the ones with the long black-and-white striped tail. Arrive early: in the morning the lemurs are active and unafraid - they sometimes come within a metre. The proceeds have funded the village school. Allow 2 hours for the small loop, accessible to families.

Drop into an Antemoro paper workshop in Ambalavao - they make a pulp from the bark of the avoha shrub, into which they embed dried flowers and leaves. The result looks like floral parchment. You can watch every stage and leave with sheets made on site.

activities.text: Guided circuit in Anja Reserve - the local guide will also show you chameleons, boas and endemic birds tucked in the rocks that you'd never have spotted alone.

visits.text: Ranomafana means 'hot water' in Malagasy - the town existed since colonial times for its thermal springs, before the 1986 discovery of a new lemur species changed everything. Patricia Wright, an American primatologist, identified the golden bamboo lemur (Hapalemur aureus) here, a species that feeds almost exclusively on young bamboo shoots containing enough cyanide to kill ten humans. The park created in 1991 to protect this habitat covers 41,600 hectares of tropical rainforest, inscribed as UNESCO World Heritage in 2007.

Enter the park with your compulsory guide first thing in the morning - the lemurs are active early. The Park Centre trail is the best adapted for observing the golden bamboo lemur and Milne-Edwards' sifaka. The park is home to a total of 12 lemur species, 115 bird species and the giraffe weevil - Trachelophorus giraffa - whose disproportionate neck is a curiosity in its own right.

activities.text: Night circuit in the park after dinner - mouse lemurs with shining eyes, woolly lemurs and multicoloured frogs become active once darkness falls. Bring a head torch, long trousers and repellent. The outing lasts 2 to 3 hours depending on the circuit. Book at the park office the same morning.

practical_info.booking: At park office, head torch mandatory

visits.text: If you're here on a Wednesday, don't miss Antsirabe's zebu market - hundreds of cattle pass through every week. The herdsmen draped in their coloured blankets, the bargaining ritual and the auctions are a spectacle in themselves. It's one of the largest zebu markets in the highlands.

Antsirabe's colonial railway station is a handsome ochre building constructed under French administration, which served as the railhead southwards. It hasn't seen much traffic for a long time, but the building itself warrants a stop.

activities.text: Visit a zebu horn workshop - Antsirabe artisans carve and polish the horn to make jewellery, combs and decorative objects. It's a local speciality you won't find anywhere else at this level of quality. Buy directly from the workshop.
Take one last pousse-pousse ride through the lanes behind the station - the neighbourhoods outside the centre are less visited and more authentic.

map_keywords: Museum of the Malagasy Academy Antananarivo

visits.text: Back to Tana - use the drive to observe the terraced rice paddies of the highlands from the road. On arrival, push open the door of the Académie Malgache Museum in the Ambohijatovo neighbourhood. The collections of manuscripts, traditional musical instruments and old maps of Madagascar are presented without frills - that's precisely what makes them credible.

Late afternoon, return to Andravoahangy market for your last purchases - silver jewellery vendors are particularly numerous late in the day. Don't leave without a hand-worked filigree silver souvenir.

activities.text: Evening stroll back in the Isoraka neighbourhood - craft shops, restaurants and small local bars rub shoulders in this more relaxed neighbourhood than the Upper Town. This is where expats and travellers at the end of their circuit meet up.

visits.text: Last day in Tana. If you have a few hours before your flight, tour the Digue craft market (Lisy) - silver jewellery, zebu horn lamps, embroidery, basketwork. It's your last chance to leave with something made in Madagascar rather than imported.

For the curious, Tsarasaotra park, an ornithological reserve right in the city, is home to ducks, herons and other endemic birds a few kilometres from the centre. Half an hour is enough and it's completely unexpected in a capital city.

activities.text: Departure to Ivato airport - allow a minimum of 90 minutes from the centre, 2 hours at rush hour. Tana traffic is unpredictable.

practical_info.booking: Allow plenty of time depending on flight time

seo.h1_title: Madagascar's Central Highlands: Culture, Crafts and RN7 Rainforests
seo.intro_paragraph: No flights, no endless sandy tracks - this loop circuit from Antananarivo crosses the royal hills of Imerina, the Zafimaniry workshops of Ambositra and the lemur forests of Ranomafana in 10 days. The most accessible circuit in Madagascar - and one of the richest on a human level.
seo.seo_hook: UNESCO royal hills, nail-free Zafimaniry crafts, Ranomafana's golden lemurs and highland rice terraces: the best of Madagascar without internal flights.
featured_list: Ambohimanga: UNESCO royal city where Malagasy people still come to make wishes
Ambositra: Zafimaniry workshops and nail-free carved wood inscribed as Intangible Heritage
Ranomafana: 12 lemur species including the golden bamboo lemur discovered in 1986
Ambalavao: village reserve with 400 ring-tailed lemurs run by locals since 1999

places_taglines.MG::antananarivo: Capital of a thousand royal hills and markets painted in red island colours
places_taglines.MG::antsirabe: Thermal town at 1,500 metres where volcanic craters cradle turquoise lakes
places_taglines.MG::ambositra: Where Zafimaniry artisans build beauty without a single nail
places_taglines.MG::fianarantsoa: Red-brick colonial upper town crowned by Madagascar's only vineyard
places_taglines.MG::ambalavao: Where 400 ring-tailed lemurs leap through a reserve protected by villagers
places_taglines.MG::ranomafana: Rainforest sanctuary where golden lemurs feast on cyanide-laced bamboo

highlights: Ambohimanga: 500-year-old UNESCO royal city, active pilgrimage site, 12-tonne stone gate
Zafimaniry: nail-free woodcraft inscribed as UNESCO Intangible Heritage since 2003
Ranomafana: 12 lemur species across 41,600 hectares of rainforest inscribed as UNESCO World Heritage in 2007
Anja Reserve: 400 ring-tailed lemurs managed by 200 villagers, a community conservation model
